---
output:
  xaringan::moon_reader:
    css: ["default", "extra.css"]
    lib_dir: libs
    seal: false
    nature:
      highlightStyle: github
      highlightLines: true
      countIncrementalSlides: false
      ratio: '16:9'
---

```{r, echo = FALSE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE}
library(tidyverse)
library(readxl)
library(stargazer)
library(kableExtra)
library(sf)
library(rnaturalearth)
library(rnaturalearthdata)
library(countrycode)

knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE,
                      eval = TRUE,
                      error = FALSE,
                      message = FALSE,
                      warning = FALSE,
                      comment = NA)
```

background-image: url('libs/Images/00-Leviathan_Cover_55.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.center[.size35[**II. How and why do governments use violence against the people inside their borders?**]]

<br>

.size45[

**Today's Agenda**: Measuring "Political Violence"

- The CIRIGHTS Data project
]

<br>

.center[.size40[
  Justin Leinaweaver (Fall 2023)
]]

???

### Prep for Class
1. ...

<br>

**SLIDE**: Today we continue our work on the first paper...




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_triangles2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.size40[.content-box-white[**Paper 1**]]

.size35[
If someone came to you with the goal of better understanding the use of political violence by governments around the world, which of the data sources that we explored in class would you recommend and why? 

Your report should introduce each source to the reader with your analysis of its strengths and weaknesses. 

Ultimately, your central argument should be a clear recommendation of which source(s) they should focus on.
]

???

### Questions on the prompt?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-light_grey.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.size50[.content-box-purple[**Sources of Political Violence Data**]]

.size35[
- The US State Department's "Country Reports on Human Rights Practices"

- Amnesty International's "Annual Country Reports"

- **The Political Terror Scale (PTS)**

- The CIRIGHTS data project's "Physical Integrity Rights"

- Varieties of Democracy's (V-Dem) "Personal Integrity Rights"
]

???

Last class we explored the Political Terror Scale (PTS)

### Key takeaways? 

### - e.g. elements of validity and reliability arguments that stood out for you?

### - How do the PTS scores supplement your reading/analysis of the source docs?

<br>

+ PTS represents expert codings by area experts (right?) with reliability checks built-in. Using these measures backs up YOUR analysis of the source docs. It's not just you interpreting them this way but backed by experts!

+ PTS enables and facilitates comparisons, but not just across countries. Your reports analyze a single country across time which is a comparison exercise. You are comparing your country to itself each year through time!

+ BUT as we noted PTS is limited. 
    + Five point scale but is it safe to assume that holds for all people in the country? White dude watching TV vs LGBTQ+ person in a protest against the government?
    + PTS focuses pretty narrowly on government violence of a particular type. Leaves out much we might argue is important to measuring political violence.




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-light_grey.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.size60[.center[.content-box-white[**For Today**]]]

.size50[
**Explore the data and codebook for the [CIRIGHTS Data project](https://www.dropbox.com/sh/t8utmzsvde8m63q/AAAs1_WIJTqXurAE5nvEKWE5a?dl=0).**

Make sure you understand the observations and variables enough to play with them in class.
]

???

### Everybody ready for today's work?

<br>

Before we dive into the data we have to evaluate its validity and reliability.

### So, let's begin in this same place we've begun with the other sources, what is the concept the CIRIGHTS project is trying to measure?

- (**SLIDE**)




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-light_grey.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle


```{r, fig.align='center', out.width='75%'}
knitr::include_graphics('libs/Images/05-2-CIRIGHTS_Logo.png')
```

<br>

.size50[
.center[.content-box-white[**"...government respect for human rights (p2)"**]]
]

???

### And why is this their focus? What is their primary data source?
- (The US State Department Country Reports on Human Rights Practices!)

- Although, note on p1 that they also use AI reports for the "Personal Integrity Rights" scores

<br>

As you can see in the ToC the CIRIGHTS project divides "respect for human rights" into four broad categories: 
1. Physical integrity rights

2. Empowerment rights and freedoms

3. Occupational/ workers’ rights

4. Justice rights

<br>

### Is this a "good" way to organize a quantitative exploration of government practices on human rights? Why or why not?

<br>

### - Are all of these instruments a valid representation of the concept?

### - Any overlap across categories or debatable instrument placements?




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-light_grey.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.center[.size35[.content-box-white[**Measuring "Physical Integrity Rights" (CIRIGHTS)**]]]

.pull-left[

.size35[
<br>
]

.size45[**1) Operationalization**]

<br>

.size45[**2) Instrumentation**]

<br>

.size45[**3) Measurement**]
]

.pull-right[

.size35[
<br>
]

.size40[
1. Disappearance

2. Extrajudicial Killing

3. Political Imprisonment

4. Torture
]
]


???

GROUPS: Make an argument about validity and reliability




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-light_grey.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.pull-left[

<br>

<br>

<br>

```{r, fig.align='center', out.width='100%'}
knitr::include_graphics('libs/Images/03_3-CIRIGHTS_Title.png')
```
]

.pull-right[
.size40[The Physical Integrity Rights Index

- Disappearance

- Extrajudicial Killing

- Political Imprisonment

- Torture
]
]

.center[.size45[Evaluate: Operationalization, Instrumentation, and Measurement]]

???

The CIRIGHTS project relies on essentially the same source documents as PTS but aims to capture more of the important variation in country behavior.

For our purposes we'll focus primarily on the elements in the Physical Integrity Rights Index.

<br>

Split class into groups (one group per category), build strengths and weaknesses lists on the board analyzing the codebook across these three categories: operationalization, instrumentation, measurement





These are the obvious choices, but I want everyone to think about their group definitions of political violence.

### Based on those definitions, should we look to include one of the other measures in this dataset?

#### - Maybe an element from the Empowerment Rights Index?

- Foreign Movement, 
- Domestic Movement, 
- Freedom of Speech, 
- Freedom of Assembly & Association, Workers’ Rights, Electoral Self-Determination, and Freedom of Religion indicators




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-light_grey.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.pull-left[

<br>

<br>

<br>

```{r, fig.align='center', out.width='100%'}
knitr::include_graphics('libs/Images/03_3-CIRIGHTS_Title.png')
```
]

.pull-right[
.size40[The Physical Integrity Rights Index

- Disappearance

- Extrajudicial Killing

- Political Imprisonment

- Torture
]
]

???

### Groups: Take a few minutes to review the "Coding Protocol" on p8 in order to evaluate the validity and reliability of these measures.

<br>

Ok, report back.

### Any concerns with validity?

### Any concerns with reliability?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-light_grey.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.pull-left[

<br>

<br>

<br>

```{r, fig.align='center', out.width='100%'}
knitr::include_graphics('libs/Images/03_3-CIRIGHTS_Title.png')
```
]

.pull-right[
.size40[The Physical Integrity Rights Index

- Disappearance

- Extrajudicial Killing

- Political Imprisonment

- Torture
]
]

???

Let's now audit the data.

Everybody focus on the three countries you analyzed on Monday. 

### Would you have coded these the same way? Why or why not?

<br>

### Findings? Takeaways?

#### - Which variables seem the easiest to code / we have the most confidence in?

#### - Which the least?

<br>

### Notes

+ disap: Disappearance. A score of 0 indicates that disappearances have occurred frequently in a given year; a score of 1 indicates that disappearances occasionally occurred; and a score of 2 indicates that disappearances did not occur in a given year.

+ kill: Extrajudicial Killing. A score of 0 indicates that extrajudicial killings were practiced frequently in a given year; a score of 1 indicates that extrajudicial killings were practiced occasionally; and a score of 2 indicates that such killings did not occur in a given year.

+ polpris: Political Imprisonment. A score of 0 indicates that there were many people imprisoned because of their religious, political, or other beliefs in a given year; a score of 1indicates that a few people were imprisoned; and a score of 2 indicates that no persons were imprisoned for any of the above reasons in a given year.

+ tort: Torture. A score of 0 indicates that torture was practiced frequently in a given year; a score of 1 indicates that torture was practiced occasionally; and a score of 2 indicates that torture did not occur in a given year.



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-light_grey.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle, center

.size70[
Is there any evidence of selection bias in the CIRIGHTS measures in 2020 (most recent year coded)? 
]

???

Let's dig into the data.

- REMEMBER, our key exercise here is to analyze the validity and reliability of the measures SO THAT later we can use them to analyze country behavior.

<br>

Use the autofilter to zoom in on 2020 only

Then scroll through and talk to me about the kinds of countries with missing data in the three measures.



---

class: slideblue, middle

```{r}
d <- read_excel("../../Data/CIRIGHTS/cirights-v1.25.22.11/CIRIGHTS V1.25.22.11/cirights_v1.22.11.25.xlsx")

d2020 <- d |>
  filter(year == 2020)

#View(d2020 |> select(country, disap, kill, polpris, tort))

# Descriptive Stats Table
x1 <- d2020 |> 
  pivot_longer(cols = polpris:kill, names_to = "Source", values_to = "Value") |>
  group_by(Source) |>
  summarize(
    N = n(),
    NAs = sum(is.na(Value)),
    Prop = str_c(round(NAs/N, 2)*100, '%'),
    #Mean = round(mean(Value, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    #StdDev = round(sd(Value, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    #Min = round(min(Value, na.rm = TRUE), 0),
    #Max = round(max(Value, na.rm = TRUE), 0)
  ) 

x1 |>
  kableExtra::kbl(align = c('l', rep('c', 3)), col.names = c("Source", "N", "Missing", "Missing (%)")) |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), font_size = 22) |>
  column_spec(column = 1:4, width = "12em")
```

```{r, fig.retina=3, fig.align='center', out.width='80%', fig.height=4.5, fig.width=9.5, eval=TRUE, cache=FALSE}
## Make a map
## Use rnaturalearth to define world map data
worldmap <- ne_countries(scale = 'medium', type = 'countries', returnclass = 'sf')

# # Identify merging country label
# guess_field(d2020$country)
# guess_field(d2020$iso_a3) # 100% genc3c, iso3c, wb
# guess_field(d2020$ciri)
# guess_field(d2020$cow) # 99.5% cown
# guess_field(d2020$polity)
# guess_field(d2020$unctry) # 98% iso3n
# guess_field(d2020$unreg)
# guess_field(worldmap$adm0_a3) #95.8% iso3c
# 
# # Add newcodes
# d2020$newcode1 <- countrycode(d2020$iso_a3, origin = "iso3c", destination = "iso3c")
# 
# worldmap$newcode1 <- countrycode(worldmap$adm0_a3, origin = "iso3c", destination = "iso3c")
#d10 <- left_join(worldmap, d2020, by = "newcode1")

# Merge
d2020_1 <- d2020 |>
  select(country, iso_a3, physint_sum, polpris:kill) |>
  mutate(
    Missing = 0
  )

d10 <- left_join(worldmap, d2020_1, by = c("adm0_a3" = "iso_a3"))

# Audit missing data
# d2020_1 |>
#   select(country, physint_sum, polpris:kill, Missing) |>
#   View()
# 
# tibble(d10) |>
#   select(name, physint_sum, polpris:kill, Missing) |>
#   filter(is.na(Missing)) |>
#   print(n = 100)
# 
# tibble(d10)
# tibble(d10) |> count(Missing)


d10$Missing[d10$name == "Kosovo"] <- 0
d10$Missing[d10$name == "S. Sudan"] <- 0
d10$Missing[d10$name == "Serbia"] <- 0


d10 |>
  mutate(
    Missing = if_else(is.na(Missing), "Missing Data", "Data Collected")
  ) |>
  ggplot() +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = factor(Missing))) +
  labs(fill = "", title = "CIRIGHTS Missing Data (2020)") +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom") +
  scale_fill_brewer(type = "div", palette = 2, direction = -1)
```

???

No significant missing data


---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-light_grey.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle, center

.size70[
How much do the measures overlap in 2020? Do we need all four sub-measures or is the index sufficient? 
]

???



---

class: middle, slideblue

.center[.size55[**Correlations Across the Measures**]]

<br>

```{r, fig.align='center'}
d2020 |>
  select(disap, kill, polpris, tort) |>
  cor() |>
  kbl(digits = 2, align = c(rep('c', 5))) |>
  column_spec(column = 1:5, width = "5em") |>
  kable_styling(font_size = 40)
```

???

Measure of linear association




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-light_grey.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle, center

.size70[
What are the most common scores across the four sub-measures?
]

???



---

class: full, slideblue

```{r, fig.align='center', fig.retina=3, fig.asp=0.618, out.width='98%', fig.width=8, cache=TRUE}
d2020 |>
  select(disap, kill, polpris, tort) |>
  pivot_longer(cols = disap:tort, names_to = "Measure", values_to = "Values") |>
  group_by(Measure) |>
  count(Values) |>
  mutate(
    Values = case_when(
      Values == 0 ~ "Score 0",
      Values == 1 ~ "Score 1",
      Values == 2 ~ "Score 2"
    )
  ) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = Measure, y = n, fill = Values)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge", color = "grey", width = .6) +
  #geom_hline(yintercept = c(25, 50, 75, 100), color = "white") +
  theme_bw() +
  #scale_fill_manual(values = c("red", "orange", "green3"))
  scale_fill_brewer(type = "div", palette = 5) +
  labs(x = "", y = "Count", fill = "",
       title = "Physical Integrity Rights",
       caption = "Source: CIRIGHTS 2020") +
  scale_x_discrete(labels = c("Disappearances", "Killings", "Political Prisoners", "Torture"))
```

???

```{r}
d2020 |>
  select(disap, kill, polpris, tort) |>
  pivot_longer(cols = disap:tort, names_to = "Measure", values_to = "Values") |>
  group_by(Measure) |>
  count(Values) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = Values, values_from = n, names_prefix = "Score ") |>
  kbl(align = 'c') |>
  column_spec(column = 1:4, width = "3em")
```



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-light_grey.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle, center

.size70[
Have the utilization rates for the types of violations varied across time?
]

???



---

```{r, fig.retina=3, out.width='97%', fig.width=6, fig.align='center', fig.asp=0.618}
## You make a line plot of averages across time for each measure
d |>
  pivot_longer(cols = polpris:kill, names_to = "Variables", values_to = "Values") |>
  group_by(year, Variables) |>
  summarize(
    Mean = mean(Values, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) |>
  ungroup() |>
  ggplot(aes(x = year, y = Mean, color = Variables)) +
  geom_line(size = 1.2) +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(x = "", y = "Mean Values",
       title = "Tracking Political Violence Averages Across Time") +
  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(0,2), xlim = c(1980, 2021)) +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = 0:2, labels = 0:2)  +
  guides(color = "none") +
  scale_color_brewer(type = "qual", palette = 2) +
  annotate("text", x = 2021, y = c(1.73, 1.2, 1.45, .65), label = c("Disap", "Kill", "Prison", "Torture"), color = RColorBrewer::brewer.pal(4, name = "Dark2"))

```

???

Remember, lower values are bad!





---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-light_grey.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle, center

.size70[
Any countries with a total index score that challenges your intuitions? 
]

???



---

class: full, middle, slideblue

```{r, fig.retina=3, fig.align='center', out.width='100%', fig.height=5, fig.width=9.5, eval=TRUE, cache=FALSE}
## Make a map
# Merge
d10 <- left_join(worldmap, d2020, by = c("adm0_a3" = "iso_a3"))

# Audit missing data
# d2020 |>
#   select(country, physint_sum, polpris:kill) |>
#   View()
# 
# tibble(d10) |>
#   select(name, physint_sum, polpris:kill) |>
#   filter(is.na(physint_sum)) |>
#   print(n = 100)

d10$physint_sum[d10$name == "Kosovo"] <- 7
d10$physint_sum[d10$name == "S. Sudan"] <- 0
d10$physint_sum[d10$name == "Serbia"] <- 6

d10 |>
  ggplot() +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = factor(physint_sum))) +
  labs(fill = "", title = "physint_sum (2020)") +
  #theme(legend.position = "bottom") +
  scale_fill_brewer(type = "div", palette = 5, na.value="darkgrey")
```

???

We'll talk about the US in a sec.

### How does this compare to the worst offenders per PTS?

### Any surprises?

<br>

Interestingly, the CIRIGHTS group do not include color codings for the US in the report they released last year.

- Let's examine the US scores together.

### what do you think of these scores for us?

```{r}
d2020 |>
  filter(iso_a3 == "USA") |>
  select(country, year, physint_sum, polpris:kill) |>
  print(n=50)
```

<br>

**SLIDE**: Let's look at the US over time



---

class: middle, slideblue

.pull-left[
.center[.size40[.content-box-white[**USA in CIRIGHTS**]]]

```{r, fig.retina=3, out.width='100%', fig.width=7, fig.align='center', fig.asp=1, cache=TRUE}
d |>
  filter(iso_a3 == "USA") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = year, y = physint_sum)) +
  geom_line(size = .4) +
  geom_point() +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(x = "", y = "Physical Integrity Rights") +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = 0:8, labels = 0:8, limits = c(0,8))
```
]

.pull-right[
```{r, fig.retina=3, out.width='80%', fig.width=7, fig.align='center', fig.asp=1.5, cache=TRUE}
## You make a line plot of averages across time for each measure
d |>
  filter(iso_a3 == "USA") |>
  pivot_longer(cols = polpris:kill, names_to = "Variables", values_to = "Values") |>
  mutate(
    Variables = case_when(
      Variables == "disap" ~ "Disappearances",
      Variables == "kill" ~ "Killings",
      Variables == "tort" ~ "Torture",
      Variables == "polpris" ~ "Political Prisoners"
    )
  ) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = year, y = Values, color = Variables)) +
  geom_line(size = .4) +
  geom_point() +
  theme_bw() +
  facet_wrap(~ Variables, ncol = 1) +
  guides(color = "none") +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = 0:2, labels = 0:2) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(1980, 2020, 4)) +
  labs(x = "") +
  scale_color_brewer(type = "qual", palette = 2)
```
]




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-light_grey.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.size55[
Focus on the scores for total index score in the years since 2001 and find us other examples of countries that have:

  + Improved the most, and
  
  + Regressed the most across the years in the sample
]

???



---

class: full, middle, slideblue

```{r, fig.retina=3, fig.align='center', out.width='100%', fig.height=5, fig.width=9.5, eval=TRUE, cache=FALSE}
## Make a map

# Summarize data
d_diff <- d |>
  filter(year %in% c(2001, 2020)) |>
  select(country, iso_a3, year, physint_sum) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = year, values_from = physint_sum, names_prefix = "physint_") |>
  mutate(
    Difference = physint_2020 - physint_2001
  )

# Merge
d11 <- left_join(worldmap, d_diff, by = c("adm0_a3" = "iso_a3"))

# Audit missing data
# d_diff |>
#   select(country, Difference) |>
#   View()
# 
# tibble(d10) |>
#   select(name, Difference) |>
#   filter(is.na(Difference)) |>
#   print(n = 100)

# d10$Difference[d10$name == "Kosovo"] <- 7
# d10$Difference[d10$name == "S. Sudan"] <- 0
# d10$Difference[d10$name == "Serbia"] <- 6

d11 |>
  ggplot() +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = Difference)) +
  labs(fill = "", title = "Changes in the Physical Integrity Index (2001 to 2020)") +
  #theme(legend.position = "bottom") +
  #scale_fill_gradient(low = "red", high = "blue")
  scale_fill_gradient2()
```

???




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-light_grey.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.center[.size55[**Measuring Political Violence by Countries**]]

.size40[
Sources: 
- Amnesty International and the US State Department

Measures:
- The Political Terror Scale (PTS)
- The CIRIGHTS Data project
]


???

### Bottom line, how reliable and valid are the measures of state political violence represented by the research projects we've explored this week?

#### - Strengths of existing sources and measures?

#### - Weaknesses of existing sources and measures?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-light_grey.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.size60[.center[**For Next Class**]]

.size50[
The V-Dem Varieties of Democracy "Personal Integrity Rights"


]

???

For Friday we repeat today's work on the CIRIGHTS data project.

Get ready to work!

The V-Dem Varieties of Democracy "Personal Integrity Rights"
- FILE 1: V-Dem/V-Dem-CY-Core_csv_v13/codebook_v13.pdf
- FILE 2: V-Dem/V-Dem-CY-Core_csv_v13/V_Dem_Personal_Integrity_Rights-2022.xlsx
Syllabus Tasks
1. Review the V-Dem Varieties of Democracy Codebook *ONLY p30, 32-34, 40-42, 176-178*
2. Audit the "Personal Integrity Rights" scores for your country cases from last week
3. Spend some time exploring the dataset and find us something interesting to discuss
